nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered areas of coral reefs, reef slopes, and lagoons; often found in turbid or partially shaded zones

decorPreferences: open space with clearance from neighbors

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae, which can supply a large portion of energy needs under adequate lighting. Benefits from weekly target feeding with meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, chopped clams, or scallops. Food should be gently squirted directly onto the vesicles using a turkey baster or pipette, ideally with flow turned off for 5–10 minutes. Has been known to regurgitate if overfed.

goodWith: other LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (15+ cm)
invertebrates with pincers or sharp appendages that may damage vesicles
high-flow species that require strong currents

summary: Bubble Coral (Plerogyra sinuosa) is a distinctive LPS coral with fluid-filled grape-like vesicles that expand by day and retract at night to reveal long sweeper tentacles. Generally considered intermediate-level; prefers low flow and low-moderate light.
careNotes:
Plerogyra sinuosa is one of the most recognizable LPS corals in the hobby, characterized by its large translucent bubble-like vesicles (pseudotentacles) that inflate during the day. At night, these retract to expose long sweeper tentacles that have been reported to reach 15 cm or more — these can and will sting neighboring corals, so adequate spacing of at least 15 cm from other specimens is strongly advised.

Key care points:
• Flow: Low to low-moderate indirect flow is generally preferred. The bubbles should sway gently; strong or direct flow has been known to prevent bubble expansion and may damage the fragile vesicle tissue. Random, non-laminar flow is preferred over direct powerhead blasts.
• Lighting: Low to moderate intensity, generally considered optimal around 50–150 PAR. Sources broadly agree the species tends to do well under lower light than many other LPS corals; it is commonly placed on the sand bed or lower rockwork. When transitioning from lower to higher light, gradual acclimation is recommended.
• Placement: Sand bed or low-to-mid rockwork is generally preferred, on a flat stable surface. The coral cannot self-right if toppled; securing with reef-safe epoxy or glue is advisable. Sediment accumulation on the skeleton should be cleared periodically with a gentle turkey baster blast.
• Feeding: Weekly target feeding with meaty foods (mysis, brine shrimp, chopped shrimp, clam) is considered beneficial for long-term health and growth. The zooxanthellae provide a substantial fraction of nutritional needs, but supplemental feeding tends to support better coloration and growth. Turn off flow during feeding to allow the food to be captured.
• Vesicle fragility: The fleshy bubbles puncture easily. Handle by the hard skeleton only. Brown jelly infection (a bacterial condition) has been reported to occur following physical trauma — inspect new specimens carefully before purchase.
• Toxin note: Some sources indicate this species may release irritants when stressed; exercise caution when performing water changes or placing it near other sensitive corals.
• Taxonomy note: The family assignment for Plerogyra is contested in the literature. Wikipedia (2026) lists Plerogyridae; several aquarium sources and more recent molecular work place it in Euphylliidae. Euphylliidae is used here as the more current assignment, but this should be verified against the current WoRMS/CoralBase record.
• Growth: Growth in captivity has been reported as slow; wild colonies can reach ~1 m across over many years, but captive specimens rarely exceed 30 cm.
• Care level sources disagree notably: ReefChasers rates this 'advanced'; AquariumStoreDepot and Fragbox rate it 'beginner'; TFH describes it as suitable for novice reefkeepers; Reeflings rates it 'moderate.' 'Intermediate' was selected as a representative average. The main risks (vesicle damage, sweeper tentacle aggression, flow sensitivity) are manageable but require attention.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs. Captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby. Fragging is generally considered impractical due to the coral's soft tissue arrangement and the difficulty of cutting through the skeleton without injuring the polyp.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – LPS Hard Corals
primary.notes: Primary source page returned HTTP 404; Bubble Coral was not listed in LiveAquaria's current inventory. Data sourced from secondary sources per playbook fallback protocol.

additional.name: AquariumStoreDepot – Bubble Coral Care: A Complete Guide
additional.notes: Used as primary data source after LiveAquaria 404. Provided temperature 76–82°F (24–28°C), pH 8.0–8.4, salinity 1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 350–450 PPM, magnesium 1250–1350 PPM, lighting 50–150 PAR, flow low-moderate, aggression/sweeper tentacles 4–6 inches (10–15 cm), care level beginner-to-intermediate, feeding meaty foods supplementally.

additional.name: Reeflings – Bubble Coral (Plerogyra sinuosa) Complete Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 24–28°C, pH 8.0–8.4, salinity SG 1.023–1.026, lighting 100–150 PAR, flow moderate. Family listed as Euphylliidae. Depth range 3–35 m confirmed.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Pearl Bubble Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked care level (rated 'advanced'), lighting 100–150 PAR, flow 'LOW', weekly spot feeding recommended, calcium 400–450 PPM, alkalinity 8–9 dKH. Adult size ~12 inches diameter. Sweeper tentacles confirmed aggressive.

additional.name: TFH Magazine – Give Your Minireef a Bit of the Bubbly
additional.notes: Cross-checked compatibility (5–6 inch clearance for sweeper tentacles), feeding (weekly with meaty foods via turkey baster), and behavior (vesicle expansion/retraction cycle). Describes species as suitable for novice reefkeepers.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Plerogyra sinuosa
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy and native range confirmation. Native range from Red Sea and Madagascar west Indian Ocean to Okinawa and Line Islands in Pacific. Colony can reach 1 m across. Low light and gentle flow confirmed. Family listed as Plerogyridae (older classification — Euphylliidae used in this record per aquarium source consensus).
